story:
Red Martian soil stained Mark Forster’s fingers as he watered the prickly aloe that lived in a terracotta pot by the window. He pressed his rough palms to the cold surface and let the quiet of the Second Post wash over him. The concrete outpost sat on a hill, its transparent panels braced against the equatorial gales that howled each night, turning the lighted box into a lone lantern on a vast, empty plain. He was the only human in this part of Mars.

The rest of the crew worked far to the north, where robots carved out a landing facility for ships due the following year. Mark had volunteered for this assignment, hungry for space after six months in transit and another half year of shared routines and shoulder-to-shoulder living with people the tests swore were the perfect company for him. He hadn’t argued with the tests, but he still chose the wind. He wanted to hear its songs.

Their mission had mapped only a small fraction of a planet too large to grasp, and Mark barely ventured beyond the sturdy skin of the Second Post. Even at noon, stepping out meant a suit he’d learned to hate on the Moon—bulky, stiff, too much between him and everything else. On one of his rare forays, he brought back a sample of Martian soil and, inside the habitat, set his aloe in it, the Earth plant nestled in red dust inside the old pot. The aloe had ridden with him in the all-terrain vehicle from the First Post. The others had dropped him on the hill and gone back, leaving Mark alone with the storms and the silence.

Sometimes he woke in the middle of the night to stand at the panoramic window, watching constellations drift on black velvet. He would stay until the pale gray of dawn bled into the sky and the aloe’s spiked leaves seemed to glow from within.

One dawn, still staring at the plant, he thought he saw it move. He leaned in. At the top of the soil, something violet pressed upward. The aloe bent, one leaf nudging into the red grit like a hand, easing the new shoot into the light. Mark’s fingers hovered over the pot, trembling, then stilled.

He lifted his gaze to the plain beyond the hill. What had been lifeless, all ochre and shadow, now shimmered with a wash of lilac. More shoots pierced the dust, brief and fragile at first, then braver, rising toward the low sky, opening into blooms beneath the far-off Sun. The wind still sang against the panels, but the song had changed.

He did not move. He watched as the Martian plants leaned toward the aloe, as if answering a call, and the aloe leaned back, as if it understood. Below, the plain woke color by color, a quiet rush of petals and stems, the first breath of a world that had waited. In the solitude he had chosen, Mark became the witness to an answer he had not thought to ask for: that on a distant hill under a thin dawn, life recognized life, and knew it was not alone anymore.
